metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or a `CLAUDE.md` / design-doc section documenting the key decisions: why `criteria_routing.json` is derived (not edited directly), why `category: code-review-pass` is separate from `review`, and how the move-catalog / `threshold_for` pattern is meant to evolve.
findings.finding: The novel architecture introduced (move-catalog pattern, `criteria_routing.json` as a derived routing index, `threshold_for` resolver, overlay `category: code-review-pass` separation) is not captured in any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update, leaving future maintainers without a rationale for why these patterns were chosen over alternatives.
findings.location: Scope section; Overlay prompt category + CI drift gate section; Coach prompt output contract section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name and specify the `criteria_routing.json` regeneration entry-point (analogous to `regenerate_prompt_index`) — define which front-matter keys it reads, what defaults it applies, and where the script lives (e.g. `python -m rebar.llm.code_review.registry regenerate-routing`) so the regenerate-no-diff drift test can be concretely written.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `criteria_routing.json` is a 'DERIVED routing index' regenerated from 'the prompts' front-matter + the registry's defaults' and promises a 'regenerate-no-diff drift test' — but never names or specifies the derivation script/command. The plan-review precedent (`criteria_routing.json`) is NOT derived from front-matter: it is a hand-authored source file. The code-review plan claims derivation (like `index.json` from prompts), but `index.json` is generated by `regenerate_prompt_index()` (a named, documented function). WS2's derivation script is unnamed and its input mapping (which front-matter keys feed which routing fields) is unspecified. Without this, the 'regenerate-no-diff' test cannot be written, and the 'DERIVED' claim is unverifiable.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json` + 'Overlay prompt category + CI drift gate' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or document as a WS3 concern) whether the existing `overlay_triggers` step will be extended to accept `changed_files` + `glob_triggers` inputs for file-path matching, or whether WS3 will use a different mechanism (e.g. extending `select_code_reviewers`) — and update the cross-story contract accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'WS3's overlay_triggers op reads [the applies_to globs from criteria_routing.json] to emit deterministic Round-A include_<overlay> flags,' but the existing overlay_triggers step (steps.py) matches keywords against text — it does NOT read file globs or match against changed file paths. File-glob matching currently lives in select_code_reviewers / prompts.select_reviewers. This is an unverified capability assertion about how the existing mechanism will be extended.
findings.location: Overlay catalog — per-id applies_to globs section; 'These globs are authored in each overlay prompt's applies_to front-matter and SURFACED into criteria_routing.json; WS3's overlay_triggers op reads them to emit the deterministic Round-A include_<overlay> flags.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the ownership model for `criteria_routing.json` mutation: either (a) the generator reads `blocking_enabled` overrides from the registry (so WS5 edits the registry, regeneration propagates the change, and the drift test stays green), or (b) certain keys are marked generator-exempt and the drift test skips them — document which and enforce it.
findings.finding: `criteria_routing.json` is simultaneously described as a "derived" artifact (regenerated from prompt front-matter + registry, with a regenerate-no-diff drift test) AND as directly mutated by WS5 ("WS5 flips exactly those two keys to True"). The ownership and update mechanism are undefined: if regeneration overwrites WS5's edits, the drift test will fail after WS5 lands; if WS5's edits are canonical, the file is no longer purely derived and the drift test is broken by design.
findings.location: Cross-story contract section; Overlay prompt category + CI drift gate section; Plan-review remediation section
findings.suggested_fix: Preferred: make the registry the single source of truth for `blocking_enabled`; the generator reads it and emits the value; WS5 edits the registry entry, not the JSON directly. The drift test then passes after WS5's registry edit, with no silent overwrites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the exact `OVERLAY_IDS` string values (e.g. `'security'`, `'performance'`, …) and the corresponding prompt-id naming convention (e.g. `'code-review-<id>'`) in the plan so AC1's 'resolve via the prompt library' contract is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `OVERLAY_IDS` enum's string value vocabulary is never enumerated in this plan. The plan says 'WS1 OWNS the closed overlay-id enum' and 'WS2 adds CONTENT' for each member, but the actual string values of `OVERLAY_IDS` are never listed — only 11 prose overlay names appear in the glob table. The `src/rebar/llm/code_review/` module does not yet exist, so there is no codebase source to verify against. An implementer of the overlay finder prompts needs to know the exact enum member strings to name each prompt file (e.g. `code-review-security` vs. `security`) and to write the `get_prompt(<overlay-id>)` call.
findings.location: Overlay catalog — per-id applies_to globs section; 'What' section; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table mapping each OVERLAY_IDS enum member string to its prompt file name (e.g. `OVERLAY_IDS.SECURITY = 'security'` → prompt id `'code-review-security'`), and confirm whether `get_prompt('code-review-security')` or `get_prompt('security')` is the resolution call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `applies_when` tag vocabulary for code moves (e.g. the overlay id strings, or a separate tag namespace) and specify the `active_triggers` source for the code-review gate's `coach()` call (e.g. which fired overlay ids, or surviving finding criteria).
findings.finding: The `applies_when` trigger-tag vocabulary for the code move-catalog is undefined. The kernel's `move_applies()` does a string-set intersection between a move's `applies_when` tags and `active_triggers` passed to `coach()`. The plan states each code move carries `applies_when` triggers but never specifies: (a) what tag strings the code moves should use, and (b) what the `active_triggers` source is for the code-review gate's `coach()` call — the plan-review gate derives them from the surviving findings' criteria ids (`{c for f in surviving for c in f.get('criteria', [])}`), but the code-review gate has a different criteria namespace.
findings.location: moves.py section; 'Coach prompt output contract + deterministic render' section; AC7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the regeneration interface for `criteria_routing.json` — name the function (e.g. `regenerate_code_review_routing()`) and its CLI entrypoint (or state it is driven by the same `regenerate_prompt_index` machinery) so the AC5 drift test is implementable.
findings.finding: The regeneration mechanism for `criteria_routing.json` — the script/command that writes the file from prompt front-matter + registry defaults — is not specified. The plan says 'regenerate-no-diff drift test' analogous to plan-review's, but plan-review's drift gate uses `python -m rebar.llm.prompts regenerate-index` (confirmed at prompts.py:701) to regenerate `reviewers/index.json`. No analogous command, function, or script is named for `criteria_routing.json`. An implementer cannot write the drift test (AC5) without knowing the regenerator's interface.
findings.location: AC5; 'Overlay prompt category + CI drift gate' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the precedence rule in `threshold_for`: state explicitly whether `blocking_enabled` is the authoritative field (read directly, `default_posture` is metadata only) or whether `blocking_enabled` is derived from `default_posture == 'blocking'` (making the new field redundant).
findings.finding: The `threshold_for` resolver's behavior when `blocking_enabled` (the new `criteria_routing.json` field) and `default_posture` (the existing field, also present in `criteria_routing.json`) give conflicting signals is undefined. The existing plan-review `_threshold_for` in `orchestrator.py` derives `blocking_enabled` from `default_posture == 'blocking'` (line 569). WS2's `criteria_routing.json` adds an INDEPENDENT `blocking_enabled` boolean alongside `default_posture`. The plan never specifies which field wins, or whether `threshold_for` should read `blocking_enabled` directly (ignoring `default_posture`) or derive it from `default_posture` (ignoring the new field).
findings.location: Cross-story contract section; Threshold-loading mechanism section; AC4; AC6
findings.suggested_fix: State: '`threshold_for` reads `blocking_enabled` directly from `criteria_routing.json` (the authoritative field); `default_posture` is display/routing metadata only and does NOT override `blocking_enabled`.' This aligns with WS5's flip of exactly `blocking_enabled` to `True`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how overlay prompt files are named so that `get_prompt(<overlay-id>)` (e.g. `get_prompt('security')`) resolves the CODE-REVIEW overlay and not the existing `security.md` reviewer — either use a prefixed id convention (e.g. id `code-review-security`, file `code-review-security.md`) and update all references, or document a separate resolution path.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that overlay prompts (e.g. `code-review-security.md` in `reviewers/`) resolve via `get_prompt(<overlay-id>)` where `<overlay-id>` is the bare overlay name (e.g. `security`), but `_packaged_prompt_files()` derives the prompt id as the file stem — so `code-review-security.md` maps to id `code-review-security`, not `security`. Calling `get_prompt('security')` would resolve the ALREADY-EXISTING `security.md` reviewer file, not the new overlay. The naming scheme for overlay prompt files versus their resolution id is unresolved.
findings.location: Scope section (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-*.md`) + Specifics pinned section (`get_prompt(<overlay-id>)` resolves) + AC line 1 (`all resolve via the prompt library`)
findings.suggested_fix: Name the overlay prompt files with a prefix, e.g. `code-review-security.md` → id `code-review-security`, and update the 'resolve via prompt library' AC and the `threshold_for`/`criteria_routing.json` key naming to use the prefixed ids consistently. Alternatively, place code-review overlays in a sibling directory (e.g. `reviewers/code_review/`) and extend `get_prompt` or add a dedicated `get_code_review_prompt` resolver.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit intra-WS2 sequencing note: implement and validate the criteria registry + `threshold_for` resolver + `criteria_routing.json` + one end-to-end overlay prompt FIRST (thin vertical slice), then scale to the remaining 10 overlays — this de-risks the infrastructure contract before the bulk prompt authoring is committed.
findings.finding: WS2 touches >13 files across ≥3 layers (prompt files, derived JSON index, Python registry/resolver, move catalog, contracts module) and carries 7 acceptance criteria spanning two structurally independent concerns — (a) authoring 11 overlay finder prompts + verify/coach prompts, and (b) building the criteria registry + threshold resolver + move catalog infrastructure — with no intra-ticket sequencing recommendation that de-risks the higher-uncertainty infrastructure piece before committing to all 11 prompts.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## Acceptance Criteria or a new ## Sequencing section, add: 'Sequence: (1) Deliver `registry.py` + `criteria_routing.json` + `threshold_for` resolver + drift test + one overlay prompt (e.g., `security`) resolving via the prompt library — validate end-to-end before proceeding. (2) Author remaining 10 overlay prompts. (3) Deliver `moves.py` + coach prompt + rendering test.' This preserves all 7 ACs while gating bulk work on a green infrastructure proof.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the WS2 overlay finder prompts for 'security' and 'tests' are intended to REPLACE, WRAP, or COMPLEMENT the existing `category: review` reviewer prompts (`security.md`, `tests.md`) — and update the Scope section to document this relationship explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan's overlay finder prompts for 'security' and 'tests' (new category: code-review-pass prompts) substantially overlap with the existing category: review prompts (security.md, tests.md) already in the reviewer catalog, which cover the same domains with similar applies_to globs. The plan does not discuss this relationship or justify why new prompts are authored rather than adapting the existing ones.
findings.location: Overlay catalog — per-id applies_to globs section + Scope section (src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-*.md)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief note to the Scope section stating the intended relationship: e.g. 'The new code-review-security.md finder prompt is purpose-built for the four-pass gate (it emits structured findings vs. the existing security.md reviewer's prose review); the existing category: review security.md remains for the single-pass code_review.py operation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the required move entries (at minimum the four named examples) with their `template` strings and `applies_when` conditions in the plan, or add an AC that asserts a minimum move count and names the mandatory entries.
findings.finding: The move-catalog entries are specified only by example ('e.g. extract-helper, add-regression-test, threat-model, add-migration-guard'), and no move template content or minimum required set is mandated. An executor must invent template bodies and decide which moves are required vs. optional, which is a non-trivial design decision not defaultable from context.
findings.location: What section ('A code Pass-4 move-catalog') and AC: 'A code move-catalog (moves.py) validates against MOVE_REGISTRY_SCHEMA at load'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or code block in the Specifics pinned section listing each mandatory move with its name, applies_when condition, and template string (e.g., `extract-helper: "Extract {subject} into a helper function to reduce duplication"`). Update AC3 to assert the four named moves are present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a calibration spike or lightweight measurement plan (e.g., a fixture with a sample diff corpus + expected overlay-trigger counts, or a stated success metric) so glob precision/recall and threshold fitness can be evaluated and iterated rather than deferred indefinitely.
findings.finding: The starter `applies_to` globs and the `(0.95, False)` threshold defaults are asserted heuristics with no measurement plan — the plan explicitly defers calibration but provides no spike, fixture, or metric that would tell the team when the defaults are wrong or when glob precision/recall is insufficient.
findings.location: Overlay catalog — per-id `applies_to` globs section; Specifics pinned section ("Threshold defaults (concrete)")

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `performance` overlay's `applies_to: ['**/*.py']` broad glob with a narrower set (e.g. `**/services/**`, `**/db/**`, `**/cache*`, `**/*query*`, `**/batch*`) that targets perf-sensitive file patterns, OR explicitly state that performance is LLM-routed (no `applies_to` glob at all, triggered only by content keywords), consistent with how WS1 routes the overlay.
findings.finding: The `performance` overlay's `applies_to: ['**/*.py']` glob fires on virtually every Python file change, making the overlay effectively always-active. Since WS3's `overlay_triggers` op reads these globs to emit deterministic `include_<overlay>` flags before the LLM runs, `**/*.py` defeats the purpose of glob-based gating: it doesn't filter to perf-relevant changes, it matches the entire codebase. Every Python code review will trigger the performance overlay, which diverges from the stated goal ('gated to perf-relevant findings by the prompt') because the gating moves to the prompt (LLM cost) rather than the deterministic glob (free). This makes `performance` behaviorally unlike all other overlays (which use narrow globs) and will inflate LLM costs on every review.
findings.suggested_fix: Either omit `applies_to` entirely for `performance` (making it LLM-only-triggered, like plan-review's perf overlays) or use a targeted set such as `['**/services/**', '**/db/**', '**/cache*', '**/*query*', '**/batch*', '**/*io*']` to match genuinely perf-sensitive paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the cross-section ambiguity by updating the 'What' section to say 'extend (not duplicate) the code_review/registry.py module WS1 created' and replacing 'parallel to plan_review/registry.py' with 'following the same pattern as plan_review/registry.py but in the same file WS1 established.'
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes the criteria registry as 'parallel to plan_review/registry.py' (implying a structurally separate, analogous file), while the 'Scope' section says 'EXTEND WS1's module: add the criteria registry' (implying a single extended file). These two framings cross-section contradict on whether code_review/registry.py is a new standalone file mirroring plan_review/registry.py, or the existing WS1 file receiving additive content.
findings.location: 'What' section ('A code-review criteria registry (parallel to plan_review/registry.py)') vs. 'Scope' section ('src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py — EXTEND WS1's module: add the criteria registry')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the What bullet to: 'A code-review criteria registry added to WS1's src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py (same file, additive changes), following the same pattern as plan_review/registry.py: per-criterion exec/applies_to/default_posture/block_threshold/blocking_enabled + the threshold_for resolver.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (or extend AC7) asserting that `code_review/contracts.py` exists, that `code_review_coach` is importable from it, and that the contract is registered in the prompt library's contract registry (mirrors the plan-review precedent check).
findings.finding: The described deliverable `code_review/contracts.py` (which registers the `code_review_coach` structured-output contract) has no AC that explicitly verifies its existence, importability, or that the contract is registered under the expected name — AC7 only asserts the coach prompt is 'authored with a registered move-pick output contract' and that the kernel renders a template, which is insufficient to catch a missing or misnamed contracts module.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC7 to: 'The Pass-4 coach prompt is authored with a registered move-pick output contract; `code_review/contracts.py` exposes `code_review_coach` and it is resolvable via `get_contract("code_review_coach")`; a test asserts the kernel `coach()` renders a picked move's template deterministically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py` to the Scope section's file list and add an AC that verifies `code_review_coach` is registered (i.e. `contracts.response_model_for('code_review_coach')` returns a valid model).
findings.finding: The `code_review/contracts.py` file is mentioned in the 'Coach prompt output contract' section ('a registered structured-output contract (`code_review_coach`, in `code_review/contracts.py`) pins that shape') but is absent from the Scope section's file list. The Scope enumerates `registry.py`, `criteria_routing.json`, `moves.py`, and the prompt files — but NOT `contracts.py`. This creates an unspecified co-location gap: an implementer who follows the Scope section will miss the contracts module, and there is no AC that directly tests `code_review_coach` contract registration.
findings.location: Scope section; 'Coach prompt output contract + deterministic render' section; AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py — registers the code_review_coach structured-output contract (mirrors plan_review/passes.py's register_contracts())' to the Scope bullet list, and extend AC7 to assert contract registration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the subjective trailing clause in AC4 with a concrete, in-session-evaluable assertion, e.g.: 'all criteria_routing.json entries default to blocking_enabled=False at WS2 ship (WS5 flips the secrets/High-Critical-security keys).'
findings.finding: AC4 appends a subjective, non-measurable qualifier — 'thresholds start high → mostly advisory in v1' — to an otherwise concrete criterion. This phrase cannot be evaluated in-session because 'mostly advisory' is undefined (does one blocking criterion violate it?).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'The code-review criteria registry exposes a threshold_for(criteria) -> (block_threshold, blocking_enabled) resolver that READS criteria_routing.json (mirrors plan_review/registry.py); thresholds start high → mostly advisory in v1.'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC4's trailing clause as: 'all entries in the delivered criteria_routing.json have blocking_enabled=False (the two keys reserved for WS5 are present but also False).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
